title: Celebrity problem (stack)
description: Find celebrity using stack elimination method

examples.explanation: Step 1: Check candidate 0. knows(0,1)=1 (disqualifies - celebrity shouldn't know anyone). Break. Step 2: Check candidate 1. knows(1,0)=0 (good), knows(2,1)=1 (good). knows(1,2)=0 (good), knows(2,1)=1 (good). All checks pass. Return 1.

Step 1: Check candidate 0. knows(0,2)=1, so 0 is disqualified. Step 2: Check candidate 1. knows(1,0)=1, so 1 is disqualified. Step 3: Check candidate 2. For j=0,1,3: knows(2,j)=0 and knows(j,2)=1. All checks pass. Return 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every pair of people (i, j) to see if i knows j and j does not know i, verifying the celebrity condition for each candidate. This is straightforward but inefficient for large n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Celebrity Identification
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution checking all possible pairs to find the celebrity.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check candidate 0. knows(0,1)=1 (disqualifies - celebrity shouldn't know anyone). Break. Step 2: Check candidate 1. knows(1,0)=0 (good), knows(2,1)=1 (good). knows(1,2)=0 (good), knows(2,1)=1 (good). All checks pass. Return 1.

Step 1: Candidate 0 is disqualified because knows(0,2)=1. Step 2: Candidate 1 is disqualified because knows(1,0)=1. Step 3: Candidate 2 passes all checks: knows(2,0)=0 and knows(0,2)=1; knows(2,1)=0 and knows(1,2)=1; knows(2,3)=0 and knows(3,2)=1. Return 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Candidate Verification
ladders.desc: Verify if a given person satisfies all celebrity conditions.

examples.trace: Check j=0: i≠j, knows(1,0)=0 ✓, knows(0,1)=1 ✓. j=2: knows(1,2)=0 ✓, knows(2,1)=1 ✓. All checks pass. Return true.
Check j=1: i≠j, knows(0,1)=1 ✗ (disqualifies immediately). Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Condition Checking
ladders.desc: Check celebrity conditions between two specific people.

examples.trace: knows(1,0)=0, knows(0,1)=1. Both conditions satisfied: i doesn't know j, and j knows i. Return true.
knows(0,1)=1, knows(1,0)=0. First condition fails: i knows j. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matrix Traversal for Pair Checking
ladders.desc: Iterate through a knows matrix to access relationship data.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves traversing the knows matrix to access relationship data between any two people. The knows matrix is typically an n×n matrix where knows[i][j] = 1 if person i knows person j, and 0 otherwise. The intuition is that we need to efficiently access these relationship values when checking pairs of people. Matrix traversal is fundamental because the brute force approach requires examining many pairs. The step-by-step process: Given the knows matrix and indices i and j, access knows[i][j] to determine if i knows j, and knows[j][i] to determine if j knows i. In the full algorithm, we'll need to traverse all pairs (i,j) where i≠j. This involves nested loops: outer loop for i from 0 to n-1, inner loop for j from 0 to n-1, skipping when i=j. Time complexity for accessing a single element is O(1), but traversing all pairs is O(n²). Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation, though the matrix itself takes O(n²) space. This connects to the pairwise checking (L2) as the mechanism to get the data needed for those checks. Common mistakes: 1) Index errors when accessing the matrix, 2) Confusing row and column indices, 3) Not understanding that knows[i][j] and knows[j][i] are different queries, 4) Inefficient traversal patterns. The real code pattern is simple matrix indexing.

Pseudocode:
# Accessing a single pair
relation1 = knows[i][j]
relation2 = knows[j][i]

# Traversing all pairs
for i in range(n):
    for j in range(n):
        if i != j:
            # Check pair (i,j)
            pass

examples.trace: Access knows[1][2]: row 1, column 2 = 0. Access knows[2][1]: row 2, column 1 = 1.

examples.input: Traverse all pairs for n=2, knows: [[1,0],[1,1]]
examples.trace: i=0, j=0: skip (i=j). i=0, j=1: process pair (0,1). i=1, j=0: process pair (1,0). i=1, j=1: skip.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing 2D Array Elements
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from a two-dimensional array using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves accessing elements in a 2D array (matrix) using row and column indices. The intuition is that data is stored in a grid format, and we need to retrieve specific values by specifying their coordinates. In programming, a 2D array is typically represented as an array of arrays, where the first index selects the row and the second index selects the column within that row. For the celebrity problem, the knows matrix is a 2D array where knows[i][j] represents whether person i knows person j. Accessing an element is a constant-time operation: given the array and indices i and j, return the value at position (i,j). The step-by-step process: 1) Validate indices are within bounds (0 ≤ i < number of rows, 0 ≤ j < number of columns), 2) Use array indexing syntax to retrieve the value. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation. This connects to matrix traversal (L3) as the fundamental operation used to get relationship data. Common mistakes: 1) Index out of bounds errors, 2) Confusing row-major and column-major order, 3) Off-by-one errors with 0-based indexing, 4) Not understanding that 2D arrays are typically arrays of references to 1D arrays. The real code pattern is straightforward indexing.

Pseudocode:
value = array[i][j]

Example in Python-style:
knows = [[1,0],[1,1]]
val = knows[0][1]  # Returns 0

examples.trace: Access row 0: [1,2,3]. Within row 0, access element at index 2: 3.
Access row 1: [1,1]. Within row 1, access element at index 0: 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of arrays and index-based access.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation level covers the fundamental concepts of arrays and index-based access, which are prerequisites for working with the knows matrix in the celebrity problem. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index. The intuition is that arrays allow us to organize data in a linear sequence for efficient access. Key concepts: 1) Arrays are zero-indexed in most programming languages (first element at index 0). 2) Each element can be accessed in constant time using its index. 3) Arrays have a fixed size (though some languages support dynamic arrays). For the celebrity problem, we use a 2D array (matrix), which is essentially an array of arrays. Understanding how to access elements using indices is crucial because the knows matrix uses indices to represent people: knows[i][j] uses i to identify the "knower" and j to identify the "known." The step-by-step process for accessing an element: Given an array arr and index i, use arr[i] to get the element at position i. For 2D arrays, arr[i][j] first gets the i-th row (which is itself an array), then gets the j-th element from that row. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity for the array is O(n) for 1D or O(n²) for 2D. This connects to accessing 2D array elements (L4) as the more specific application. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking arrays are 1-indexed, 2) Index out of bounds errors, 3) Confusing the array itself with its elements, 4) Not understanding that indices must be integers. The real code pattern is simple indexing.

Pseudocode:
# 1D array
arr = [10, 20, 30]
first = arr[0]  # 10
second = arr[1] # 20

# 2D array
matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]]
val = matrix[0][1]  # 2

examples.trace: Array has elements at indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15, 3→20. Index 2 corresponds to value 15.
First access row 1: [3,4]. Then within that row, access element at index 0: 3.

approaches.name: Stack Elimination O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to hold all people, repeatedly pop two, eliminate one based on the 'knows' relation, and push the potential celebrity back. Finally, verify the last candidate. This leverages stack LIFO to efficiently narrow down possibilities.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Celebrity Using Stack Elimination
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the celebrity problem using stack elimination method.
ladders.explanation: The celebrity problem asks us to find a person (if exists) who is known by everyone but knows nobody else in a party of n people. The stack elimination approach provides an O(n) time solution by systematically eliminating non-celebrities. The intuition is that we can compare pairs of people using the 'knows' relation to eliminate one person from each pair, gradually narrowing down to a single candidate. This works because if person A knows person B, A cannot be the celebrity (since celebrities know nobody), and if A doesn't know B, B cannot be the celebrity (since everyone must know the celebrity). We use a stack to manage this elimination process efficiently. First, we push all n people onto a stack. Then we repeatedly pop two people, compare them using the 'knows' relation, eliminate one, and push the potential candidate back. After n-1 comparisons, we have one candidate left. Finally, we must verify this candidate by checking two conditions: 1) The candidate knows nobody, and 2) Everyone knows the candidate. If both conditions hold, we return the candidate; otherwise, return -1. The time complexity is O(n) because we make n-1 comparisons during elimination and up to 2n-2 comparisons during verification. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. This approach is elegant because it reduces the problem size by half with each comparison round, similar to tournament elimination. Common mistakes include forgetting the verification step (the final candidate might not be a true celebrity), incorrectly handling edge cases like n=0 or n=1, and misunderstanding the 'knows' relation direction. The stack's LIFO property ensures we process people in a consistent order, making the algorithm deterministic.

examples.trace: Initialize stack [0,1,2]. Pop 2 and 1: knows(2,1)=1, eliminate 2, push 1 -> [0,1]. Pop 1 and 0: knows(1,0)=0, eliminate 0, push 1 -> [1]. Candidate=1. Verify: knows(1,0)=0 and knows(1,2)=0; knows(0,1)=1 and knows(2,1)=1. Return 1.

Stack: [0,1,2,3]. Pop 3 and 2: knows(3,2)=0 → 3 doesn't know 2, eliminate 2, push 3 → [0,1,3]. Pop 3 and 1: knows(3,1)=1 → 3 knows 1, eliminate 3, push 1 → [0,1]. Pop 1 and 0: knows(1,0)=0 → 1 doesn't know 0, eliminate 0, push 1 → [1]. Candidate=1. Verify: knows(1,0)=0 ✓, knows(1,2)=0 ✓, knows(1,3)=0 ✓. knows(0,1)=1 ✓, knows(2,1)=1 ✓, knows(3,1)=1 ✓. All pass, return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Elimination Process
ladders.desc: Implement the core elimination logic that reduces n people to one candidate using a stack.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the celebrity problem solution. The intuition is to treat the problem as a tournament where we eliminate players based on pairwise comparisons. We start with all n people in a stack. While there are at least 2 people in the stack, we pop two (call them A and B), and use the 'knows' relation to decide who to eliminate. If A knows B, then A cannot be the celebrity (since celebrities know nobody), so we eliminate A and push B back. If A doesn't know B, then B cannot be the celebrity (since everyone must know the celebrity), so we eliminate B and push A back. This process continues until only one person remains in the stack, who becomes our candidate. The key insight is that this elimination preserves the potential celebrity: if a celebrity exists, they will never be eliminated because they never know anyone (so they won't be eliminated in the 'A knows B' case) and everyone knows them (so they won't be eliminated in the 'A doesn't know B' case). The algorithm is O(n) time because we perform exactly n-1 comparisons. Space is O(n) for the stack. This feeds into the full problem by providing the candidate, which then needs verification. Common mistakes include popping in wrong order (should be LIFO), not handling the stack correctly after elimination, and misunderstanding the elimination logic. The pseudocode: stack = list of 0 to n-1; while len(stack) > 1: a = stack.pop(); b = stack.pop(); if knows(a, b): stack.append(b); else: stack.append(a); candidate = stack.pop(). This ensures we always compare the most recently added pairs, which is efficient.

examples.input: n=4, knows matrix as in L0 example 2
examples.trace: Stack init: [0,1,2,3]. Iteration1: pop a=3, b=2, knows(3,2)=0 → push a=3 → stack=[0,1,3]. Iteration2: pop a=3, b=1, knows(3,1)=1 → push b=1 → stack=[0,1]. Iteration3: pop a=1, b=0, knows(1,0)=0 → push a=1 → stack=[1]. Candidate=1.
Stack: [0,1,2]. Iter1: pop a=2, b=1, knows(2,1)=1 → push b=1 → stack=[0,1]. Iter2: pop a=1, b=0, knows(1,0)=1 → push b=0 → stack=[0]. Candidate=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Elimination Rule
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the rule for eliminating one person from a pair using the 'knows' relation.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental decision rule that drives the entire algorithm. Given two people A and B, we need to determine which one to eliminate based on whether A knows B. The intuition comes from the definition of a celebrity: a celebrity knows nobody, and everyone knows the celebrity. Therefore, if A knows B, then A cannot be the celebrity (since they know someone), so we eliminate A and keep B as a potential candidate. Conversely, if A does not know B, then B cannot be the celebrity (since not everyone knows them—specifically, A doesn't), so we eliminate B and keep A. This rule is symmetric and ensures that if a celebrity exists in the pair, they will never be eliminated. For example, if A is the celebrity, then A doesn't know B, so we eliminate B and keep A. If B is the celebrity, then A knows B (since everyone knows the celebrity), so we eliminate A and keep B. The time complexity for this single comparison is O(1), and it uses no extra space. This feeds into the stack elimination process by being applied repeatedly to pairs. Common mistakes include reversing the logic (e.g., eliminating B when A knows B), or not considering that the 'knows' relation might not be transitive. The pseudocode is simple: if knows(A, B): return B (keep B); else: return A (keep A). This rule is the core insight that makes the O(n) solution possible.

examples.trace: Since A knows B, A cannot be celebrity (knows someone), so eliminate A, keep B.
Since A doesn't know B, B cannot be celebrity (not known by A), so eliminate B, keep A.

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Pair Management
ladders.desc: Implement stack push, pop, and size operations to manage a list of people.
ladders.explanation: This level involves using a stack data structure to hold and process people. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) collection, which is ideal for this problem because we repeatedly take the top two elements, compare them, and push one back. The intuition is that by using a stack, we can efficiently access the most recently added pairs without needing to rearrange the entire list. We start by pushing all n people onto the stack (e.g., in order 0 to n-1). Then, in a loop, we pop two people, apply the elimination rule, and push the survivor back. This continues until only one person remains. The stack ensures that we always compare the latest pair, which simplifies the algorithm. Time complexity for stack operations is O(1) per push/pop, and overall O(n) for n operations. Space is O(n) to store the stack. This feeds into the elimination process by providing the mechanism to handle pairs. Common mistakes include using a queue instead of stack (which would change the order), not checking stack size before popping, or incorrectly initializing the stack. Pseudocode: stack = []; for i in range(n): stack.append(i); while len(stack) > 1: a = stack.pop(); b = stack.pop(); result = elimination_rule(a, b); stack.append(result); candidate = stack.pop(). This builds directly on the pairwise rule from L2.

examples.trace: Start: stack=[0,1,2]. Pop a=2, b=1 → apply rule (assume knows(2,1)=1) → keep 1 → push 1 → stack=[0,1]. Pop a=1, b=0 → apply rule (assume knows(1,0)=0) → keep 1 → push 1 → stack=[1]. Candidate=1.
Start: [0,1,2,3]. Pop 3,2 → rule → push survivor (assume keep 2) → [0,1,2]. Pop 2,1 → rule → push survivor (assume keep 1) → [0,1]. Pop 1,0 → rule → push survivor (assume keep 0) → [0]. Candidate=0.

testCases.input: n=2, operations: init, pop two, push one

ladders.title: Basic Operation — 'Knows' Relation Check
ladders.desc: Implement a function that checks if person A knows person B given a matrix.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental operation in the celebrity problem. The 'knows' relation is represented as a 2D matrix where knows(A, B) = 1 if A knows B, and 0 otherwise. The matrix is typically n x n, and we assume it's provided as input. The intuition is that this relation defines the social network: it's not necessarily symmetric or transitive. For example, A might know B but B might not know A. To check if A knows B, we simply access matrix[A][B]. In code, this is a direct array lookup. Time complexity is O(1), and no extra space is needed. This operation feeds into all higher levels because every decision depends on it. Common mistakes include indexing errors (e.g., using matrix[B][A] instead of matrix[A][B]), not handling edge cases like A or B out of bounds, or misunderstanding the matrix representation. Pseudocode: def knows(A, B, matrix): return matrix[A][B]. This function is used repeatedly in the elimination rule and verification. It's crucial that the matrix is correct and consistent; for example, matrix[i][i] is often 1 (a person knows themselves), but this doesn't affect the celebrity definition since we only care about knowing others.

examples.trace: matrix[0][1] = 0, so A doesn't know B.
matrix[1][0] = 1, so A knows B.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Learn how to represent and access data in a 2D matrix (list of lists).
ladders.explanation: A 2D matrix is a grid of values arranged in rows and columns, commonly represented in programming as a list of lists. For example, a 3x3 matrix can be [[1,0,1],[0,1,0],[1,0,1]]. Each inner list represents a row, and elements within are columns. To access an element, we use two indices: matrix[row][column]. The first index selects the row, and the second selects the column. In the context of the celebrity problem, the matrix represents the 'knows' relation: matrix[i][j] = 1 if person i knows person j, and 0 otherwise. The intuition is that this is a compact way to store pairwise relationships between n people. For a beginner, think of it as a table where rows are people and columns are people they might know. Time to access an element is O(1). Space is O(n²) to store the matrix. This foundation feeds into the 'knows' check operation. Common mistakes include confusing rows and columns, using 1-based indexing (programming typically uses 0-based), or not understanding how to iterate over the matrix. Pseudocode: matrix = [[1,0],[0,1]]; value = matrix[0][1] # gets 0. This is essential for all higher levels because the entire problem relies on this data structure.

examples.trace: Row 0 is [1,2], column 1 is the second element, which is 2.
Row 1 is [8,9,10], column 0 is the first element, which is 8.

approaches.name: Two Pointer Elimination O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain two pointers (left and right) to traverse the list, comparing and eliminating one person at a time based on the 'knows' check. This avoids extra space for a stack while still achieving linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Celebrity Identification with Two-Pointer Elimination
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the celebrity problem using two-pointer elimination in O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: The celebrity problem asks us to find a person in a group of n people who is known by everyone but knows nobody else. We're given a helper function knows(a, b) that returns true if person a knows person b. The two-pointer elimination approach solves this efficiently without extra data structures. The intuition is that we can maintain two pointers (left and right) that traverse from opposite ends of the person list, comparing and eliminating one person at each step based on the 'knows' relationship. If left knows right, left cannot be the celebrity (since celebrities know nobody), so we move left forward. If left doesn't know right, right cannot be the celebrity (since everyone must know the celebrity), so we move right backward. This elimination continues until only one candidate remains. We then verify this candidate by checking if they know nobody and everyone knows them. The algorithm works because at each comparison, we eliminate at least one person who cannot be the celebrity based on the definition. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize left = 0, right = n-1. 2) While left < right: if knows(left, right): left++ else: right--. 3) Candidate = left (or right, they're equal). 4) Verify candidate: Check that candidate knows nobody (for all i, !knows(candidate, i) unless i == candidate) and everyone knows candidate (for all i, knows(i, candidate) unless i == candidate). 5) Return candidate if valid, else -1. Time complexity is O(n) for elimination plus O(n) for verification = O(n). Space is O(1). This connects to the key subroutine by implementing the complete solution. Common mistakes include not verifying the candidate properly (both conditions must be checked) and off-by-one errors in the verification loop. Real code pattern: def findCelebrity(n): left, right = 0, n-1; while left < right: if knows(left, right): left += 1 else: right -= 1; candidate = left; for i in range(n): if i != candidate and (knows(candidate, i) or not knows(i, candidate)): return -1; return candidate.

examples.input: n=4, knows matrix: 0 knows [1,2], 1 knows [2], 2 knows none, 3 knows [2]
examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3. knows(0,3)=false (0 doesn't know 3), so right-- to 2. Step 2: left=0, right=2. knows(0,2)=true (0 knows 2), so left++ to 1. Step 3: left=1, right=2. knows(1,2)=true, so left++ to 2. Now left=2, right=2, loop ends. Candidate=2. Verification: Check knows(2,0)=false, knows(2,1)=false, knows(2,3)=false (2 knows nobody). Check knows(0,2)=true, knows(1,2)=true, knows(3,2)=true (everyone knows 2). All pass, return 2.

examples.input: n=3, knows matrix: 0 knows [1], 1 knows [0], 2 knows none
examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=2. knows(0,2)=false, so right-- to 1. Step 2: left=0, right=1. knows(0,1)=true, so left++ to 1. Now left=1, right=1, loop ends. Candidate=1. Verification: Check knows(1,0)=true (fails because celebrity should know nobody), so return -1. Even though everyone knows 1 (knows(0,1)=true, knows(2,1)=true), 1 knows 0, so not a celebrity.

testCases.input: n=2, knows matrix: 0 knows [1], 1 knows none
n=2, knows matrix: 0 knows none, 1 knows [0]
n=3, knows matrix: 0 knows [1,2], 1 knows [2], 2 knows none
n=3, knows matrix: 0 knows [1], 1 knows [2], 2 knows [0]
n=4, knows matrix: 0 knows [3], 1 knows [3], 2 knows [3], 3 knows none
n=4, knows matrix: 0 knows [1,2,3], 1 knows [2,3], 2 knows [3], 3 knows none
n=5, knows matrix: 0 knows [4], 1 knows [4], 2 knows [4], 3 knows [4], 4 knows none
n=5, knows matrix: all know none
n=5, knows matrix: 0 knows [1,2,3,4], 1 knows [2,3,4], 2 knows [3,4], 3 knows [4], 4 knows none

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two-Pointer Elimination Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core elimination process that reduces candidates to one using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer approach, where we efficiently narrow down potential celebrities from n candidates to just one through pairwise comparisons. The intuition is based on the celebrity definition: if person A knows person B, A cannot be the celebrity (since celebrities know nobody); if A doesn't know B, B cannot be the celebrity (since everyone must know the celebrity). By comparing two people at opposite ends and eliminating one based on this logic, we guarantee that the remaining candidate could potentially be the celebrity, though verification is still needed. The algorithm: Initialize left pointer at 0 and right pointer at n-1. While left < right, compare knows(left, right). If true, increment left (eliminate left as candidate). If false, decrement right (eliminate right as candidate). When left equals right, that person is the potential celebrity. This works because each comparison eliminates at least one person who cannot be the celebrity, preserving the invariant that the celebrity (if exists) remains among the candidates. Time complexity is O(n) as we make n-1 comparisons in worst case. Space is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the candidate, which then needs verification. Common mistakes include using while left <= right (which can cause infinite loop or incorrect elimination) and not updating pointers correctly. Real code pattern: def eliminateCandidate(n): left, right = 0, n-1; while left < right: if knows(left, right): left += 1 else: right -= 1; return left.

examples.trace: Start: left=0, right=3. knows(0,3)=false → right=2. left=0, right=2: knows(0,2)=true → left=1. left=1, right=2: knows(1,2)=true → left=2. left=2, right=2, loop ends. Return 2.
Start: left=0, right=2. knows(0,2)=false → right=1. left=0, right=1: knows(0,1)=true → left=1. left=1, right=1, loop ends. Return 1.

testCases.input: n=5, knows: 0 knows 4=true, 1 knows 3=false, 1 knows 4=true, 2 knows 3=true

ladders.title: Core Logic — Celebrity Elimination Rule
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the rule that eliminates candidates based on 'knows' checks.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental elimination logic that makes the two-pointer approach work. The core insight is derived directly from the celebrity definition: a celebrity is known by everyone and knows nobody. From this, we derive two elimination rules: 1) If A knows B, then A cannot be the celebrity (because a celebrity knows nobody). 2) If A does not know B, then B cannot be the celebrity (because everyone must know the celebrity). These rules are mutually exclusive and exhaustive for any pair (A, B). The intuition is that by comparing any two people, we can always eliminate at least one of them as a potential celebrity, never both (since the celebrity could be one of them). This guarantees progress toward a single candidate. The algorithm for a single comparison: Given persons i and j, check knows(i, j). If true, eliminate i; if false, eliminate j. This is applied repeatedly in the two-pointer loop. Time complexity per comparison is O(1), assuming knows() is O(1). Space is O(1). This feeds into the sub-routine by being the decision mechanism in each iteration. Common mistakes include misapplying the rules (e.g., eliminating both or neither) or not understanding why these rules are correct. Real code pattern: def eliminateOne(i, j): if knows(i, j): return j # i eliminated, j remains else: return i # j eliminated, i remains.

examples.trace: Rule: If A knows B, A cannot be celebrity. Here A=0, B=1, knows(0,1)=true, so eliminate 0. Return 1 as remaining candidate.
Rule: If A does not know B, B cannot be celebrity. Here A=2, B=3, knows(2,3)=false, so eliminate 3. Return 2 as remaining candidate.

testCases.input: i=1, j=1, knows=true (same person, though unusual)

ladders.title: Building Block — Two-Pointer Traversal Pattern
ladders.desc: Implement a two-pointer traversal that moves from opposite ends toward the center.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces the two-pointer technique, a common algorithmic pattern where two indices traverse a data structure from different directions. In this context, we use it to efficiently compare and eliminate elements. The intuition is that by starting at the extremes (left at beginning, right at end), we can systematically reduce the search space without extra memory. The algorithm: Initialize left = 0, right = n-1. While left < right, perform some operation comparing or processing elements at these pointers, then update pointers based on the result (typically left++ or right--). The loop terminates when left == right, meaning we've narrowed to one element. This pattern is efficient because it processes elements in linear time and constant space. Time complexity is O(n) as each pointer moves at most n steps. Space is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by providing the framework for applying elimination rules repeatedly. Common mistakes include incorrect loop condition (using <= instead of <, which can cause extra comparisons or errors), not updating pointers correctly, or off-by-one errors in initialization. Real code pattern: def twoPointerTraversal(n): left, right = 0, n-1; while left < right: # Process knows(left, right) or other logic; # Update left or right; return left.

examples.input: n=4, process: print left and right each step
examples.trace: Start: left=0, right=3 (0<3 true). Step 1: Process, assume we increment left: left=1, right=3. Step 2: 1<3 true, process, increment left: left=2, right=3. Step 3: 2<3 true, process, increment left: left=3, right=3. Now 3<3 false, loop ends.

examples.input: n=3, process: decrement right each step
examples.trace: Start: left=0, right=2 (0<2 true). Step 1: Process, decrement right: left=0, right=1. Step 2: 0<1 true, process, decrement right: left=0, right=0. Loop ends.

testCases.input: n=4, update left twice then right

testCases.expected: left=0,right=5; left=1,right=5; left=2,right=5; left=3,right=5; left=4,right=5; left=5,right=5

testCases.input: n=10, update left and right equally
testCases.expected: left=0,right=9; left=1,right=8; left=2,right=7; left=3,right=6; left=4,right=5; left=5,right=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Boolean Comparison with knows() Function
ladders.desc: Use the knows() function to compare two people and make a decision.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic operation of calling the knows() helper function and using its boolean result to guide logic. The knows(a, b) function is a black box that returns true if person a knows person b, false otherwise. In the celebrity problem, this function encapsulates the social network relationships. The intuition is that this boolean value provides critical information about directional relationships between people. The algorithm involves: Call knows(i, j) for two indices i and j. Based on the return value (true or false), execute different code paths. For example, if true, take one action; if false, take another. This is a fundamental conditional operation in programming. Time complexity is O(1) per call (assuming knows() is O(1)). Space is O(1). This feeds into the building block by being the core comparison in two-pointer traversal. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the direction (knows(a,b) is about a knowing b, not mutual) or not handling the boolean result correctly. Real code pattern: if knows(i, j): # Action for true case else: # Action for false case.

examples.trace: Call knows(0,1) returns true. Since condition is true, execute the code inside the if block (e.g., print '0 knows 1').
Call knows(2,3) returns false. Since condition is false, execute the code inside the else block (e.g., print '2 does not know 3').

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Looping
ladders.desc: Work with array indices and basic loops to traverse a list of people.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers the basics of representing n people as indices 0 to n-1 in an array or list, and using loops to iterate through them. The intuition is that in programming, we often model a group of items using sequential indices, allowing easy access and manipulation. Here, each person is represented by an integer index from 0 to n-1, where n is the total number of people. The algorithm involves: Initialize a variable n for the number of people. Use a for loop to iterate from 0 to n-1 (e.g., for i in range(n):). Within the loop, you can perform operations on each person i. This is essential for later steps where we need to check all people. Time complexity for traversing all n people is O(n). Space is O(1) if no extra data structures. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the indices to pass to knows(). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(1,n) instead of range(n)) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: n = 5; for i in range(n): print(i) # prints 0,1,2,3,4.

examples.trace: Set n=3. Start loop with i=0: print 0. i=1: print 1. i=2: print 2. Loop ends as i reaches 3 (not less than 3).
Set n=1. Start loop with i=0: print 0. Loop ends as i reaches 1.
